title: How would you update embeddings when source documents change frequently, without causing stale or duplicate retrievals?
prompt:
How would you update embeddings when source documents change frequently, without causing stale or duplicate retrievals?

*Answer in the editor as you would out loud in an interview (a few sentences to a short paragraph is fine). Then use 💡 Hint, 🧠 AI feedback, or 📗 Show answer.*
reference_solution:
Implement an atomic shadow-ingestion workflow to prevent stale RAG retrievals or downtime during index synchronization. Priority webhook queue: listen to source change APIs (Confluence, Google Drive, etc.) and enqueue updates prioritized by document query traffic, so hot documents get refreshed first. Shadow ingestion: parse, chunk, and embed the updated version into the vector store marked with status `active` — without touching or removing the old version yet. Atomic swap and supersede: delete the old vector records and update the corresponding SQL chunk rows to status `superseded` within a single transaction window, so the cutover is atomic rather than gradual. The correctness guardrail: always filter vector queries on `status = active` so queries never return duplicate or half-deleted chunks during the update window.

**Takeaway:** Never update embeddings in place — ingest the new version alongside the old under a different status, then atomically swap and supersede, with every query filtered to active status so an in-flight update is never visible as a partial or duplicate result.
hint_notes: Shadow-ingest the new version as `active`, then atomically delete-old/supersede-old in one transaction. Every query filters on status=active so mid-update states are never visible.
